California is so big, Southern California and Northern California are already two worlds. I'm half a Southern Californian, so I wouldn't be like you when talking about someone in CA would know.

I've lived in both Norcal and Socal so I have experienced both places. When my family lived in Socal, my family used to have an annual pass to Disneyland and we would go after school some days. I grew up in Norcal and I remembered being introduced to words like hella or hecka which is slang for very/a lot. A differnce between Norcal and Socal is how we refer to freeways. Socal people put "the" before every freeway name: the 405, the 5, the 101, the 210, the 57, etc. Norcal people do not so even though US 101 is in both parts of the state, Norcal people call it 101. Because I lived in Socal first, I tend to call all the freeways that exist in Socal with "the" before it. So I may say 80, 680, 92, but I say the 101, the 5.

Also something particular about CA: No one here calls it Cali. Only non-Californians ever call it that. We just call it California or refer to a specific region in our state.

According to ur impression/observation, how general American think about HK's situation comparing to BLM ? or do they compare them at all ?

How do you think Trump / Biden will take HK matters ? What kind of approaches/actions you deem is more likely to happen after or right before the white house swipe.

There is not much comparison between BLM and the situation in HK in the American society. I being a Hker and American do so. I see the parallel of police brutality killing the people they are suppose to protect. Police in the US also tried to suppress protests across the US using tear gas, rubber bullets, and pepper spray like in HK.

I study history at university so looking at US history and the constitutional process there is little chance Trump wins at this point (we would need a repeat of 1876). I think what Biden will do is try to work with US allies globally to restrain China. He may not confront China as directly but I think he will try to get the UK, EU, Canada, Australia, Japan, etc working together. The sanctions on Carrie Lam and other HK officials will remain. quote.msg:
Hey, I appreciate your views on those political/cultural/economic issues, which sound quite reasonable.

What do you think about the Quantitative easing policy adopted since Obama's administration ? Does it really work? In general how would you describe the US economy between 2010-2019 (before the pandemic burst out) ?

replies.msg:
Let me clarify one thing: The US Federal Reserve is politically independent though the federal reserve board is nominated by the president. This is why as an economics student I do not think it is good for the president to tell the federal reserve to lower interest rates because it may reduce the firewall between politics and the central bank.

On Quantitative Easing itself I think it is ok to use as an option of last resort. During the Great Recession there was so much financial friction that even when the federal funds rate was at 0, the interest rate for borrowers remained much higher. The central bank buying more long term bonds basically makes governmental bonds more scare raising the prices which lowers the returns on the bonds. If bonds have less return it leads to interest rates across the economy dropping and makes it easier to invest and lend. Normally this could lead to massive inflation but in times of a major recession like now or 2008 there is a fear of potential deflation. The other monetary policy option is to reduce the reserve ratio (the amount of money a bank must keep on hand) but this can risk the bank failing. I think it is more important to use fiscal policy than relying solely on QE. Tax cuts and government spending can increase aggregate demand.

The US economy was relatively stable and I think if it wasn't for Covid Trump wins re-election, Presidents during economic booms tend to win re-election. I think there were fundamental issues: worker pay has stagnated while CEO pay along with stocks have gone up. The recovery from the Great Recession has been uneven.


In the case of HK note that traditional monetary policy is not an option because the HKD is pegged to the USD. HK has to import US interest rates to maintain the peg.

quote.msg: Someone said „Bernie Sanders is the Democrat version of Donald Trump.“ Do you agree with that?

replies.msg:
In some ways yes. Sanders has a populist message, Trump has a populist message. Sanders is a left wing populism emphasising economics and socioeconomic class (the rich are not paying their fair share). Trump's right wing populism emphasises economics and race/nationality (immigrants are taking your jobs). Both run against elites though from different angles.

I would argue that Trump's policies are not very populist though on economic issues. His tax cut is very standard Republican economics and have helped wealthy Americans more than middle or working class Americans. His rhetoric does not match his policies.

This does not even take into account the fact that people in poorer neighborhoods have less money most likely.

quote.msg: Bro, I am also originally from HK and my family moved to US. I guess you came to US younger than I was, since you can't read chinese. I came here after finishing 5th grade. I went back to hong kong three times. Each time I went back, everything like the street & shops change a lot, I don't even recognize it. It's like you have this weird feeling of familiarity and unfamiliarity at the same time.

Yea I did all of my K-12 schooling in the US. I moved to the US like a month before starting Kindergarten (HK equivalent of K3, kindergarten in the US is 1 year not 3 years). I've only gone back 2 times but HK seemed to have changed very fast. The MTR map seems to grow everytime and new buildings go up.
When my family visited HK once, my parents were trying to find the KCR to go to the NT and one of their friends had to tell them the KCR no longer existed and was part of the MTR. The things that don't change are the crowds and that everyone is in a hurry. I say that as a compliment as I enjoy squishing on the MTR at rush hour and I think Americans are too lazy at times. There is nothing like dashing across the cross platform for transfers at admiralty, Mong Kok, Prince Edward, Yau Tong, etc.
You enjoy crowds? Haha......I am exactly the opposite, I like to chill more. What bring you to LIHKG, btw?

replies.msg:
Depends on the situation but I love that HK is a city that is always moving and people are always on the go.
I want to have discussions with HKers and understand their views and the best way to do that is to post on a website that is frequented by HKers. My parents don't even keep up with HK issues/news anymore so my family is rather disconnected to HK current events. I was talking about Joshua Wong to my parents and they don't even know who he is, they probably only know older people like Martin Lee and Emily Lau.
My other hope in posting in LIHKG is that HKers can at least see the perspective of what Americans on the left think and I hope that because I have some understanding of HK issues, I can make some connections between both. Americans yelling at HKers is not helpful.

quote.msg: Someone said „Bernie Sanders is the Democrat version of Donald Trump.“ Do you agree with that?

In some ways yes. Sanders has a populist message, Trump has a populist message. Sanders is a left wing populism emphasising economics and socioeconomic class (the rich are not paying their fair share). Trump's right wing populism emphasises economics and race/nationality (immigrants are taking your jobs). Both run against elites though from different angles.

I would argue that Trump's policies are not very populist though on economic issues. His tax cut is very standard Republican economics and have helped wealthy Americans more than middle or working class Americans. His rhetoric does not match his policies.

Thanks for your detailed answer

Haven’t you slept yet?

replies.msg: I just woke up right now.  CA is 16 hours behind HK when we are not on daylight savings time so it was not that late when I wrote you a response.  I tend to sleep around 1-2am.

For some ABCs that faced racism the preference to be white is simply a desire to avoid racial attacks. I grew up in a very Asian area and basically only had ABC friends so I never felt like we were inferior or awkward.

我呢就冇價值判斷（點敢得罪Democrat支持者）亦冇笑9佢英文更冇咩優越感。全部係你穿鑿附會諗出嚟嘅，正如我冇質疑過佢係咪abc 一樣(呢個都係你講嘅，之後重屈9我）。

Pathetic 我認㗎（之前都講咗我「低賤」㗎啦，你重想點啫（一笑））。

Hmm
But do you agree to the policies that he HAS put in place and they are helpful?
Do you agree to the fact that he helped made peace in the middle east?

Perhaps a little. But I do not think they are the most helpful solution. Imagine you have a restaurant and people don't come because your food tastes bad. You invest some money to renovate the restaurant and make the website look nicer. Perhaps more people will come to eat now because the decor is better and your website looks more professional. But, since the food sucks a nicer restaurant isn't going to fix much. This is how I see Trump's policies. They do not get at the root of the issue.

As far as the middle east, Trump tore up the Iran deal and they now tons of uranium so I think that is a danger to the middle East.

It sounds to me that the disatisfaction comes from different perspective on how to tackle an issue.

For example, I could be dissatisfied by all the benefits CA are doing, i dont think they tackle much of the root problem or that it ends up with more fraud than help, but it has been working for a long time and I believe the same dish cant please all tongues.

That's why voting is so important and the integrity of it is so important.
If there has been any fraud or cheater, whether or not it change the result, it is unacceptable. It's like a dangerous driver could get a ticket without having actually caused an accident on the road.

replies.msg:
It does indeed but I think overall his policies have hurt minorities than helped them. The opportunity zones and the criminal justice are quite limited in their positive impact and his harmful policies have a much larger impact.

Continuing to allow police brutality and his "tough on crime policies" hurt minorities significantly. Even though whites and blacks use drugs at about the same rates, blacks are significantly more likely to be arrested and charged for drug crimes. The rhetoric around drug addiction has been to criminalize them. Only with the recent opioid epidemic which effects predominately white communities has there been discussions about rehabilitation. Trump's ban on implicit bias training for federal employees limits ways to reduce biases in the way people view minorities. CA only recently has become liberal. As recent as the 1990s CA was very much at the front of an anti-immigrant and "tough on crime" wave.

quote.msg:
If you look at previous posts I mentioned that I'm gay.
Probably multiple reasons. Most of my friends growing up are ethnically Chinese and even at university I only have a few white friends. So I am generally just more comfortable being around other Asians in general. There is still a cultural difference between Asian-Americans and White Americans even if we all speak English and live in the same country.

I think ABC has their own distinct hybrid culture, from my own experience, they usually hang out with their ABC friends, only very few Chinese FOB friends or some all-American friends.

So people tend to be attracted to the group of people they belong to.

I can relate to the situation to South Asian Hong Kong, they seems to form their own community and culture, which is not entirely Hong Kong or South Asian.

Yes ABCs have a distinct culture which is why we tend to form social circles separate from that of more recent ethnically Chinese immigrants as well as white Americans. I wish that ABCs were willing to bridge the gap with newer immigrants instead of just decrying them as "FOB."  There are some areas where I do identify with my HKer friends more than my ABC friends so I consider myself a hybrid but leans more on the ABC side. I have to remember to not complain about Carrie to my ABC friends.

replies.msg:
I had very bad experience with ABC, since I’m in NYC, all ABC I met in real life or online forum are of mainlander heritage and have more sympathy for Mainland China, particularly in last year. Some Hong Konger called them blue ABC or simply “lefttard”.

And they have very weird fantasy for mainland China, to the degree that my mainlander FOB friends joked that they are more patriotic to communist regime than local mainlanders. They thought they can make a fortune in Mainland China, and received a biased massage from their mainlander parents about Hong Kong.

Very few ABC are sympathetic to the cause of Hong Konger, while they yells and fights for the cause of BLM, you can see example of MC Jin.
When I criticise their narrow views and weird fantasy, I got blamed as a rude guy. So I gradually gave up my efforts to know ABC friends and have very low regards to them. My ABC classmates only asked me how to type Chinese in wechats and giggled at my accents. At least, I have some mainlander friends respecting my point of view.


I think you heard of Facebook group, like subtle Cantonese trait, subtle Hong Kong trait. I got blocked by those admins, when I posted something about Anti-extradition movement.

In order to blend into environment in NYC, there are two paths: either joining the mainlander FOB group or ABC group, I’m comfortable with befriending with Hong Kong Fobs, fully embracing my Hong Kong side.

Why do people prefer Biden over Sanders? Is he too economically left for most Americans?

Yea they fear that Sanders is too far left.  I actually prefer Sanders to Biden.  Sanders will actually change the economic system to help people.  HK and the US both have very capitalistic systems which hurt the working class.  For instance, large supermarkets getting wage subsides in HK is from my perspective ridiculous, the coupon books they are suppose to give out  in return even more of a joke. Meanwhile, the HK Gov won't give another 10,000 HKD cash handout.  This is an example of why I think there needs to be an economic restructuring and Sanders would do it.

replies.msg:
I think Sanders tried to replicate the Scandinavian welfare system in US, but he forgot Scandinavian people had North Sea oils and countries like Norway, are racially homogenous, social consensus are more easier to form.

I will prefer Sanders definitely, if he waive the student loans I had. But I don’t think he is any closer to working class people in mid-West, he is more appealing to millennials in bicoastal blue cities. But something Trump and Sanders are in common, both are against the TPP, for which trade deal will allow the inflow of products and services from Asia.

replies.msg:
I can't speak about the NY ABC population but generally in the US we have 2 major waves of Chinese immigration
1. Pre-1965: These are the Chinese that came for things like the CA gold rush or to build the railroad. These are poor people from Guangdong with many coming from the Tiashan area.
2. Post 1965: This group is more wealthy and affluent. Educated families came mostly from HK and Taiwan at first and eventually Mainland China.
All of my ABC friends with immigrant parents are from Taiwan or HK and may identify with the political situation in both places. My ABC friends that have their origins in the first Chinese immigration wave often times do not have any relative that was born in Guangdong as their ancestors have been in the US for generations. These ABCs may identify with Chinese culture but feel no ties to the CCP, do not care about the political situation in Asia (and many only speak English).
As I have friends in both spheres, I don't feel like I perfectly fit in either. I can't relate to all of the experiences my HK friends have and I cannot speak Cantonese, but I resent my ABC friends lack of engagement on issues affecting the "homeland," and lack of willingness to interact with newer Asian immigrants.

Note that housing in the US is quite a bit cheaper, like the total rent for my 2 bedroom apartment is $2,100 USD (16,380 HKD).

replies.msg:
I purposefully didn't write shorthand since people here are most likely English Language Learners so perhaps it is a little clunky. I don't edit my posts so I'm sure my posts have some grammar errors.
Here is a sample of my academic writing. This is a research paper about one of my relatives on how their life is intertwined with CA History.

" Social attitudes towards Chinese-Americans began to change with the American entry into World War II. Previously, “[public health service] officers readily conflated the Chinese race and the spread of bubonic plague.” However, when the United States declared war on Japan, it quickly allied itself with China, who was already fighting against Japanese incursions in mainland China. My [relative name removed] explained that Chinese-American social standing improved because of the United States-China alliance against Japan and how the American entry into World War II was a “fortunate” outcome for Chinese-Americans. One Chinese-American remembered how in ‘“the 1940s for the first time Chinese were accepted by Americans as being friends…Chinese and Americans were fighting against the Japanese.”’ People no longer saw Chinese-Americans as undesirable and untrustworthy but rather as people that could be a part of American society and support American interests. While Japanese-Americans dealt with internment camps, White Americans embraced Chinese-Americans and granted new employment opportunities to them. “Many college-educated Chinese were now able to find employment in their fields of training…Chinese men employed…in professional and technical occupations increased from 2.5 percent in 1940 to 6.6 percent in 1950.” If a Chinese-American male was able to receive an education, they now had the possibility of working in a white-collar job rather than being confined only to menial jobs that society deemed acceptable for Chinese-Americans to work in. [My relative name removed] could still not access these professional jobs due to only having a high school diploma and not a college education. World War II changed social attitudes towards Chinese-Americans and provided opportunities for educated Chinese-Americans. Another war would be necessary for [relative name removed] to have the means to obtain his undergraduate degree in electrical engineering- the Korean War."

The UN was broken before China. The UNSC has 5 permanent members with vetos: US, UK, France, China, and Russia (the Allies from WW2). During the Cold War, the US and the Soviets would just veto anything in the UNSC. The UNHRC is kinda useless but it is a way for the US to exert some influence.

quote.msg: Do you think localists are far right?

replies.msg:
Not necessarily. Localist is also a broad term. Are some further to the right? Yea. Again I think there is a need to preserve HK culture and localists bring many grievances to light. You might be surprised but I actually like localists such as Edward Leung and Ray Wong. They are willing to fight for HK when the pan dems would not. They are willing to talk about issues the pan dems won't discuss (e.g. parallel trading with the baby formula). I support the localists use of more confrontational tactics.
I think I disagree at what the solutions to the problems they identify. I may disagree with the pro-independence stance of some of them but I respect their right to hold that position. The solutions are more left. Something like parallel trading is caused by businesses exploiting the lack of regulation of products on the Mainland. The solution is to remove the capitalist framework that exists on both sides of the borders. Ideally, Mainlanders should be able to buy safe baby formula on their side of the border. Also, one can promote a HK identity while still advocating for the democratic development of China- it's not mutually exclusive.

replies.msg:
Illegal immigrants pay taxes just like any other California resident. If they pay into the system they are entitled to access the benefits available to any Californian. Why does CA not have enough money? A major issue is the way we do property taxes. Prop 13 limits property taxes to 2% of the property value at purchase plus 1-2% growth in price per year. Property taxes are not accessed at market rate which limits revenue in a state like CA which has high property values. The accessed value of property taxes does not even grow at the rate of inflation.

Californian voters in this election voted against reforming prop 13 to allow expensive properties to be taxed at market rate rather than the Prop 13 rate.

I think communism is probably not achievable though I do think some elements of socialism can be possible. Communism won't happen because people are selfish though it would be great if we as people could do that. In Hong Kong you have the Hospital Authority which provides low cost health care and is run by the government.

quote.msg: After watching the news conference hosted by Trump lawyers, I think the US elections are a mess and they are susceptible to frauds.

replies.msg:
As I have stated before, I believe in the integrity of the US election system. GA has significant voter restrictions and is controlled by Republicans yet Biden still won. GA even did a hand recount and while they did find some ballots that forgot to be tallied, these did not change the overall outcome. GA is also the sate with the closest margin between Biden and Trump. I welcome proof by Trump's lawyers but almost all of the cases have been thrown out of court. It is of most importance that the elections are safe and their integrity is maintained.

Trump's only chance to victory is to convince Republican legislatures in MI, WI, and PA to declare that Trump won and have them legislatures send their own electors to Washington DC for the electoral college vote. Congress would have to decide which group is the legitimate one. The historical precedent would be 1876 when there were allegations of fraud and voter suppression in 3 states: SC, FL, and LA. I do not think 2020 is the same as 1876. Trump also does not have to cooperate with the transition of power if he does not want too- Herbert Hoover did the same in 1832 and there is no Constitutional obligation to do so.

You can read more about the 1876 election here https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/1876_United_States_presidential_election. If you have questions about 1876 vs 2020 you can ask me as well.

quote.msg: Do you think localists are far right?

Not necessarily. Localist is also a broad term. Are some further to the right? Yea. Again I think there is a need to preserve HK culture and localists bring many grievances to light. You might be surprised but I actually like localists such as Edward Leung and Ray Wong. They are willing to fight for HK when the pan dems would not. They are willing to talk about issues the pan dems won't discuss (e.g. parallel trading with the baby formula). I support the localists use of more confrontational tactics.
I think I disagree at what the solutions to the problems they identify. I may disagree with the pro-independence stance of some of them but I respect their right to hold that position. The solutions are more left. Something like parallel trading is caused by businesses exploiting the lack of regulation of products on the Mainland. The solution is to remove the capitalist framework that exists on both sides of the borders. Ideally, Mainlanders should be able to buy safe baby formula on their side of the border. Also, one can promote a HK identity while still advocating for the democratic development of China- it's not mutually exclusive.

I think one of the blind spots of the leftist view of HK problems is that it could not identify that China is colonizing Hong Kong, and in a much more brutal way than British colonialism ever was. Some of the leftists still consider HKers more privileged than Chinese immigrants (see Minnie Li).
The UK never thought of completely destroying the language and culture of HK, while China not just only does that but also actively makes HK's welfare goes to the Chinese immigrants.

replies.msg:
I think we can romanticize the British colonial history. They suppressed labor movements and red-lined various parts of the colonies for whites. Chinese was not an official language until 1974 and the first direct elections for LegCo came in 1991 with the abolition of appointed seats in 1995. Many of the colonial laws such as the requirement to receive a letter of no objection or sedition are now used by the HK gov to supress speech.

Of course the CCP are repeating similar things to the British depriving universal suffrage and using colonial laws to reign in the city's autonomy. It is obvious the HK gov gives preferences to the Central government as well as more recent mainland immigrants. One only has to look at things such as the push to use Mandarin as a Medium of Instruction in Chinese language classes. Forcing Mandarin on the city is no different than the British forcing English on HK.

As I have stated before, I believe in the integrity of the US election system. GA has significant voter restrictions and is controlled by Republicans yet Biden still won. GA even did a hand recount and while they did find some ballots that forgot to be tallied, these did not change the overall outcome. GA is also the sate with the closest margin between Biden and Trump. I welcome proof by Trump's lawyers but almost all of the cases have been thrown out of court. It is of most importance that the elections are safe and their integrity is maintained.

Trump's only chance to victory is to convince Republican legislatures in MI, WI, and PA to declare that Trump won and have them legislatures send their own electors to Washington DC for the electoral college vote. quote.msg:
Not necessarily. Localist is also a broad term. Are some further to the right? Yea. Again I think there is a need to preserve HK culture and localists bring many grievances to light. You might be surprised but I actually like localists such as Edward Leung and Ray Wong. They are willing to fight for HK when the pan dems would not. They are willing to talk about issues the pan dems won't discuss (e.g. parallel trading with the baby formula). I support the localists use of more confrontational tactics.
I think I disagree at what the solutions to the problems they identify. I may disagree with the pro-independence stance of some of them but I respect their right to hold that position. The solutions are more left. Something like parallel trading is caused by businesses exploiting the lack of regulation of products on the Mainland. The solution is to remove the capitalist framework that exists on both sides of the borders. Ideally, Mainlanders should be able to buy safe baby formula on their side of the border. Also, one can promote a HK identity while still advocating for the democratic development of China- it's not mutually exclusive.

I think one of the blind spots of the leftist view of HK problems is that it could not identify that China is colonizing Hong Kong, and in a much more brutal way than British colonialism ever was. Some of the leftists still consider HKers more privileged than Chinese immigrants (see Minnie Li).
The UK never thought of completely destroying the language and culture of HK, while China not just only does that but also actively makes HK's welfare goes to the Chinese immigrants.

I think we can romanticize the British colonial history. They suppressed labor movements and red-lined various parts of the colonies for whites. Chinese was not an official language until 1974 and the first direct elections for LegCo came in 1991 with the abolition of appointed seats in 1995. Many of the colonial laws such as the requirement to receive a letter of no objection or sedition are now used by the HK gov to supress speech.

Of course the CCP are repeating similar things to the British depriving universal suffrage and using colonial laws to reign in the city's autonomy. It is obvious the HK gov gives preferences to the Central government as well as more recent mainland immigrants. One only has to look at things such as the push to use Mandarin as a Medium of Instruction in Chinese language classes. Forcing Mandarin on the city is no different than the British forcing English on HK.

replies.msg:
Many of the European countries teach English as their second language in public school. I think you have some misunderstanding about the colonial times Hong Kong and probably see the English teaching is a bad policy of colonial empire. Actually its not.

I don't know what do you want from local HKers, but from your words, you are trying to convince HKers that colonization is evil and British & CCP are in the same level. I am 100% sure no one could agree colonization is good, and of course british did unforgivable things before, but at least they brought some reforms to the society and that's not harmful to our cultural roots.

I am able to talk to you in English because of the English schools. I have a family background with very long history, english didn't destroy any of my heritage. I can still keep my traditions and beliefs.

And even the history textbooks in colonial times mentioned the whole colonization thing. So the reality is not what you imagine.

quote.msg:
To be honest, I believe universal healthcare and social care are basic civil right to let its citizens to have a decent life. But the most of the Republicans are worried that kind of policies are harmful to market system as the cost might be very huge.
At the same time, collective healthcare also is not a good idea to US because I personally believe that the collective concept of social support network will become a new political power to limiting individual rights of Americans. You can see what happening in Hong Kong if their employees rebel against the unpopular system.

HK has very limited legal protections for employees and this is because of the capitalist system.   Private insurance is very costly and Americans have turned to "Go Fund me" donations to pay for expensive medical procedures.  This is not a sustainable system.  A Medicare for all proposal would just affect the insurance- all doctors would still be private.

correct, so I think the public opinion maybe will changed on universal healthcare after this Chinese virus pandemic. While private insurance companies still are the preferable choice for individual needs, provide public healthcare to American citizens and legal residents is not a bad idea at all.

replies.msg: That would be what we would call a public option.  HK's system allows for private insurance while also providing a public option for those who cannot afford it. I think a full government paid system is better but I am totally content with moving to a system where there is a public option for those who do not have private insurance.

quote.msg:
People here believe Biden is inclined to show a more friendly / tolerant approach to China, attempting to convince it to develop democracy, which has proved to be ineffective.

As far as I know the Biden's campaign enjoyed funds from Big Techs. I guess they will push him to make it easier for them to continue their business with China. In return China will probably request US to leave aside those issues in HK or Xinjiang. That's what concern many of us. What do you think about that ?

I think that is very much a concern. I think something that will give us some clue on Biden's direction will be his cabinet picks. He supposedly has chosen his secretary of state but has not publicly revealed it yet. It is not going to be Susan Rice as Republicans hate her and will block her appointment.
Big tech putting profits over human rights is a concern for me and is part of a larger reason why I think capitalism allows exploitation by putting profits over humanity. I do expect Biden to try to re-open some trade in China but I also think Biden very much wants to contain China as it threatens the Western control of the world. At the end of the day, the US wants to preserve its status as a superpower so Biden will move to do that. India is a growing market and perhaps the US will try to encourage American investment there as a replacement.

In case you are wondering about the twitter+FB fact checking, the 1st Amendment only applies to the government and not private businesses. Social media is free to censure any person they wish to provided its stated in their terms and conditions of service. I also think big tech companies are growing way too powerful and need to be broken up. The US did this to AT&T's Bell Systems in the 1980s.

I don't think capitalism is the part of human right exploration, because nobody can limiting high-tech companies to stop them having relationships with hostile power like China and radical leftism in US.

replies.msg:
High tech companies in the US are not radical leftists. They are corporations so inherently leftist. I grew up right to Silicon Valley so let me explain what I am saying.
CA is quite socially liberally but I think in many ways it is still rather capitalist economically. Silicon Valley remains a place of private enterprise and innovation. Businesses in Silicon Valley exploit their workers for profits like any other corporation. Californian voters still refuse to touch Proposition 13 which sets limit on the amount of property taxes people pay.

replies.msg: I think PC culture is a good thing in general.  We should be respectful to people and inclusive.  As a gay person I appreciate it when people don't assume I have/I want a girlfriend.  In American English it is common to use "their" as a singular third person even though its plural third person. We use it because it doesn't specify a gender and is more inclusive.  A classic SAT question is to use their/they/them in a singular way as it is technically incorrect.

replies.msg:
The circumstances Europeans nations adopted teaching English and HK under colonial rule are different situations. Europeans need a language to communicate with each other so English serves that purpose. Until recently, the UK was part of the EU as well. No European country is making English the only official language in their country or being forced by the UK to teach English. There are other ways to teach English besides colonizing an entire territory. Yes I agree one can learn English without loosing their culture but that is contrary to the very basic nature of a colony. I'm not against EMI schools provided the students attending EMI schools have an adequate level of English.

Yes the British colonial government did many good things at the end of the time but a lot of these were in response to the instability of the 1967 riots. Things like the MTR, public education, public healthcare, civil liberties, Chinese as an official language, etc are all after the 1967 riots.

The wrongs of the British colonial govt does not negate what China is doing today. China is more powerful than the British were at the end of the colonial era and as a result is able to exert its influence a lot more. The challenge for HK now is how to deal with a colonial power on the rise rather than on the decline.
any way to know foreign friends ?
Do you want to Make America Great Again?

quote.msg: any way to know foreign friends ?

replies.msg:
I think one has to be able to listen to different ideas and get to know people. If they speak a foreign language it means either you or the foreigner has to be willing to communicate in their non-native tongue and the native speaker has to be patient sometimes. Also willing to learn and understand different cultures and experiences. Avoid the hot button political issues unless you know them well, like I won't talk about HK issues/politics with my mainland friends.
The foreigners also have to be willing to interact with locals but our job is to be as welcoming and inclusive as we can.

quote.msg: Do you want to Make America Great Again?

replies.msg:
The short answer is no.

The long answer is I do not think there was a period I would describe America as "great." America is powerful but that doesn't mean good. China is powerful does that mean it is great? The US has a legacy of racism and oppression and if anything the US needs to build a truly equitable society. We also have a large history of imperialistic ambitions that I wish to not continue. Our economy also exploits working class people for the enrichment of the rich.
That doesn't mean the US has never done good things or the US has never contributed to the world.
